text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, tilted to the right. The surgeon exposes the fourth part of the duodenum and incises the peritoneum. Dissection follows the dorsal aspect of the duodenum medially to laterally, reaching the dorsal hepatic flexure cranially and the cecum caudally. Traction and counter traction are applied carefully to avoid trauma to mesofacial structures. Toll's fascia is incised along the duodenal rim to dissect the ventral aspect of the uncinate process, preserving the fascia on the dorsal mesocolon for protection.

The surgeon completes the dorsal dissection by dividing the medial peritoneal fixation of the cecum. The patient is repositioned to a right side up anti-Trendelenburg position, and the robotic system is redocked. The V-shaped configuration of the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels is identified, representing critical view number two, and the mesentery is incised at the angle between these vessels.

An additional grasper is used to exert traction on the terminal ileum through the lid of an Alexis retractor inserted via the Pfannenstiel incision. This technique avoids injury to the mesocolon along the course of the ileocolic vessels. The dorsal dissection plane is entered, providing an excellent view of the pancreatic head and a 180-degree axis to the superior mesenteric vessels, which are safely dissected. The lymphatic tissue around the ileocolic pedicle and surgical trunk is preserved and excised en bloc with the specimen.

The surgeon follows the superior mesenteric artery cranially, which is typically located on the right side of the superior mesenteric vein. A half circumferential resection of the lymphatic tissue is performed, facilitated by the mobility and accessibility gained from previous dorsal dissection. The ileocolic vein and artery are divided between clips placed using the assist port, with the sequence of critical views three and four adjusted based on the topography of the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon applies small metal clips to vessels, ensuring both distal ends of the applicator are visible. The transverse mesocolic page is separated from the mesogastric page by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, preserving the gastromental vessels. The division begins left of the midline to access the lesser sac, leaving omental adhesions to the right colon and tumor area intact. The dissection crosses the second part of the duodenum and extends into the hepatocolic ligament, reaching the dorsal dissection plane. The mesocolon is separated from mesogastric structures along an avascular plane.

The surgeon completes the division of rhizocolic adhesions to the duodenum, demonstrating critical view number six with the mesogastric page retracted cranially and the transverse mesocolic page caudally. The right branch of the middle colic artery is dissected using an inframesocolic approach, with cranial retraction of the mesocolon. Due to limited visibility of the artery branches, a perpendicular incision is made directly anterior to the superior mesenteric vein, revealing the right branch as a horizontal vessel originating from the middle colic artery at the base of the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon demonstrates critical view number seven by exposing the origin of the right branch of the middle colic artery to ensure arterial perfusion of the transverse colon. The right branch is divided between clips using a vessel sealer, while carefully avoiding injury to the accompanying right colic vein. The gastro-mental vein is spared due to prior isolation of mesogastric structures, and other veins running into the gastrocolic trunk of Henle are divided. The instrument is passed behind the superior right colic vein running into the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.

The surgeon completes the central dissection and divides the mesocolon and omentum, ensuring the perfusion of the remaining colon for safe anastomosis. The colon is then divided using a stapler, followed by careful lateral dissection to avoid lesions of the mesocolon or tumor. The intracorporeal division of the mesoelium is performed with attention to maintaining an adequate distance from the tumor's lymphatic drainage area.

The surgeon divides the mesoelium using a vessel sealer, followed by the division of the ileum with a robotic stapler. An intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis is performed using a 60mm blue cartridge. The opening is closed with two barbed sutures starting from each end of the stapler line, crossing halfway to create a two-layer suture line.
Specimens were classified as type zero A in both patients and postoperative causes were also uneventful for both. Thank you very much for watching.
